coredata.dc:title: Multifunctional carbon dot anchored halloysite nanotube: Nanovehicle for cisplatin drug release, cytotoxicity on breast cancer cells and DNA binding studies
coredata.dc:creator: Prashanth, M.K.
Chaitra, K.
Krishnaiah, Prakash
Prashanth, K.N.
Yogesh kumar, K.
Kumar, Sandeep
Alharthi, Fahad A.
Parashuram, L.
Raghu, M.S.
coredata.dc:description: Halloysite nanotube (HNT) is a clay material paving much attention in the field of biological studies. In the present work multifunctional carbon dot (CD) has been synthesized and functionalized with HNT that resulted in HNT-CD nanocomposite. Fabricated HNT-CD nanocomposite has been subjected to analytical and morphological characterization using XRD, FTIR, SEM and EDX techniques and indicates proper functionalization of CD with HNT. Cisplatin has been evaluated for drug loading efficiency for HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite and found encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity is high for the nanocomposite. Cisplatin release studies with HNT and HNT-CD have been conducted at different pH. Cisplatin loaded with HNT and HNT- CD was further evaluated for in-vitro cytotoxicity test against MCF-7 cells. Cisplatin-loaded HNT- CD nanocomposite showed greater toxicity against MCF-7 cell lines compared to HNT alone. HNT-CD nanocomposite was further evaluated for its capacity to interact with Ct-DNA using UV–visible, Fluorescence spectrophotometry and circular dichroism using spectropolarimeter. The ∆G value for the HNT-CD was found to be 27.91 KJ mol−1 indicates a spontaneous binding process with Ct-DNA. The obtained n value close to 1, suggests the existence of a single binding site between the HNT-CD hybrid and Ct-DNA. The circular dichroism results are in good agreement with findings of the absorption and emission studies and indicate association with intercalative binding mode of HNT-CD hybrid with Ct-DNA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: HNT-CD
Cytotoxicity
MCF-7 cells
Fluorescence
Circular dichroism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Functionalization of HNT with multifunctional carbon dots (HNT-CD)
Synthesis of cisplatin loaded functionalized HNT-CD (cis@HNT-CD)
of encapsulation efficiency (EE) and loading capacity (LC)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors are immensely elated and wish to express their indebted gratitude to the Management of their respective Institutes for providing lab facilities to carry out this work. Authors extend their thanks and appreciation to Researchers Supporting Project (Ref: RSP-2021/160) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. Also, this research was supported by Basic Science Research Programthrough the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Education (2021R1A6A1A03043682).

Authors are immensely elated and wish to express their indebted gratitude to the Management of their respective Institutes for providing lab facilities to carry out this work. Authors extend their thanks and appreciation to Researchers Supporting Project (Ref: RSP-2021/160) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. Also, this research was supported by Basic Science Research Programthrough the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Education (2021R1A6A1A03043682).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD of CD, HNT and HNT-CD.
FTIR of CD, HNT-CD and Cis@HNT-CD.
3a) SEM of HNT, 3b) STM of CD and 3c) FESEM of HNT-CD.
Energy Encapsulation (EE) and loading capacity (LC) of HNT and HNT-CD.
Effect of pH on Cis release in presence of 6a) HNT and 6b)HNT-CD.
Microscopic images of MCF-7 cell lines in presence of 7a) HNT, 7b) CD, 7c) HNT-CD, 7d) Cis@HNT-CD and 7e) Cell viability in presence of different materials at different concentrations.
Absorption spectra of HNT-CD hybrid (100 µM) in the absence and presence (10–80 µM) of Ct-DNA at pH 7.4 and at room temperature, Inset: plot of [DNA]/(εa-εf) Vs [DNA].

9a) Fluorescence emission spectra of EB-DNA in absence and presence (20–100 µM) 9b) Stern-Volmer plot for interaction of HNT-CD hybrid with Ct-DNA at 298 K, 9c) Plot of log(FO-F)/F vs. log[Q] of HNT-CD hybrid.
The CD spectra of Ct-DNA (50 µM) with two different concentration of HNT-CD.
Various mathematical modeling for cisplatin loaded HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite.

head.ce:title: Multifunctional carbon dot anchored halloysite nanotube: Nanovehicle for cisplatin drug release, cytotoxicity on breast cancer cells and DNA binding studies

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Halloysite nanotube (HNT) is a clay material paving much attention in the field of biological studies. In the present work multifunctional carbon dot (CD) has been synthesized and functionalized with HNT that resulted in HNT-CD nanocomposite. Fabricated HNT-CD nanocomposite has been subjected to analytical and morphological characterization using XRD, FTIR, SEM and EDX techniques and indicates proper functionalization of CD with HNT. Cisplatin has been evaluated for drug loading efficiency for HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite and found encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity is high for the nanocomposite. Cisplatin release studies with HNT and HNT-CD have been conducted at different pH. Cisplatin loaded with HNT and HNT- CD was further evaluated for in-vitro cytotoxicity test against MCF-7 cells. Cisplatin-loaded HNT- CD nanocomposite showed greater toxicity against MCF-7 cell lines compared to HNT alone. HNT-CD nanocomposite was further evaluated for its capacity to interact with Ct-DNA using UV–visible, Fluorescence spectrophotometry and circular dichroism using spectropolarimeter. The ∆G value for the HNT-CD was found to be 27.91 KJ mol−1 indicates a spontaneous binding process with Ct-DNA. The obtained n value close to 1, suggests the existence of a single binding site between the HNT-CD hybrid and Ct-DNA. The circular dichroism results are in good agreement with findings of the absorption and emission studies and indicate association with intercalative binding mode of HNT-CD hybrid with Ct-DNA.

ce:section.ce:para: Modern medicine ultimately goals at proper drug delivery at the target, personalized cures and localized disease treatment, which is certainly depending on the precise understanding of fundamental biochemistry involved in it. Due to the unconditional growth of malignant cells, cancer is the one that invades further parts of the body. Every year cancer reported cases are rising at an average of 10 million, World Health Organization projected ∼13 million deaths by the year 2030 [1]. Various types of cancers are existing across the globe, among them, breast cancer is the second most common in women next to lung cancer [2]. Scientists across the globe are understanding the biology of tumor cells and improving the treatments by proper diagnosis using sophisticated devices and thus resulting in a decrease in the mortality rate [3,4]. Radiation therapy, chemotherapy, surgical intervention, or all these combined to treat cancer. Conventional chemotherapy works on the death of fast-rising cancer cells by interfering with the mitosis and synthesis of DNA. However, those agents are capable of damaging even normal healthy organs and tissues and lead to unintentional and unwanted side effects, which is the foremost cause for the high death rate of cancer patients [5,6]. These drugs exhibit poor bio-accessibility to tumor tissues, require high doses and result in enhanced toxicity in regular cells. So, it is in high demand to develop chemotherapeutics that will particularly target cancer cells either actively or passively and ultimately improve the therapeutic efficiency by decreasing its side effects. The researchers used several useful materials including inorganic carriers, biomacromolecular scaffolds, lipids, polymers and clay materials [7–14]. In the development of drugs for cancer, DNA is one of the best targets for the development of specific drug candidates for cancer by overcoming drug resistance. It has been well documented that several different antitumor drugs produced their effects through DNA binding. The DNA interacting molecules are usually bound to DNA non-covalently by three modes such as intercalation, groove binding and static electronic interactions.

To address these challenges, halloysite nanotubes (HNTs), a clay mineral having aluminosilicate and a tubular hollow structure in the submicron range with an inner lumen diameter of 15 nm and length of 0.5 - 1 µm. The presence SiO2 at the outer surface and aluminum dioxide at the inner surface of HNTs, making it possess positively charged lumen at the inner surface and negatively charged outer surface. The lumen of HNT is exhibiting a property of binding with DNA, chemotherapeutics, RNA, growth factors and antibiotics for easy carriage and release. These HNTs are eco-friendly, economical and available in plenty, good biocompatibility with a lower level of toxicity, making them a potential candidate for delivering biologically important molecules. Functionalization of the outer silica surface could certainly help for the binding of biologically important heterocyclic molecules. However, HNT may slightly hinder its application in drug binding due to lower interaction between organic drug molecules and the inorganic surface of it. Hence it is preferred to functionalize HNT and to generate a hybrid structure by combining with multifunctional organic molecules and thereby increase the efficiency of HNTs in sustained release of drugs and cytotoxicity studies.

At present, carbon dots (CDs), because of their far-fetched adaptability considered as emerging materials in material science research possessing an average diameter <10 nm and spherical in nature. The advantage of CDs over Graphene Quantum Dot (GQD) is observed in its carbon network and its hybridization. CDs are comprised of both sp2 and sp3 hybrid carbon networks in comparison to GQDs, which possess only sp2 -hybridized carbon framework. The functional groups present on the surface of CDs like COOH and NH2 helps for the easy binding with both organic and inorganic molecules. From the literature survey, it is confirmed that CDs are the materials possessing good optical, electrochemical, electrocatalytic, bio-imaging, sensor etc [15,16]. CDs also find applications in the medicinal field as they are exhibit low or no toxicity and also possess the property of inhibiting human insulin fibrillation [17]. Another most attractive property of CDs is the efficient suppression of liver cancer cells (Hep G2), breast cancer cells (MCF-7 and MDA-MB-23) probably due to the generation of highly reactive oxygen species [18].

By keeping the above-mentioned criteria of any material to be used in biological applications authors successfully functionalized halloysite nanotube with multifunctional carbon dots. Synthesized materials are characterized and used for drug release studies of cisplatin after loading it to HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite and studied its encapsulation efficiency and loading capacity. HNT and HNT-CD were evaluated for in-vitro cytotoxicity studies against MCF-7 cell lines. HNT-CD nanocomposite has been further used for DNA binding studies. All the obtained results indicated the functionalization of HNTs with multifunctional CD could serve as a multipurpose material having importance in the field of gene therapy.

Cisplatin was received as a gift sample from Cipla.MCF-7 human Adenocarcinoma breast cancer cell lines were obtained from NCCS Pune. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM), Fetal Bovine Serum (FBS), MTT Reagent from Himedia. Dimethyl Sulfoxide (DMSO), 1-Dodecylpyridinium Chloride, CH2Cl2, Halloysite nanotubes were procured from Sigma Aldrich Mumbai. All other reagents used were of analytical grade. Ultrapure MilliQ Water was used throughout the experiments.

A 15 mM aqueous solution of 1-Dodecylpyridinium Chloride (DPC) was mixed with 2.0 M NaOH allowed for the process of carbonization DPC overnight at room temperature without any external energy. The carbonization process was monitored and the formation of CDs was confirmed by the brown-black coloration of the reaction mixture. Later, the pH of the solution is neutralized (pH=7) by the addition of HCl. After the reaction, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) was added to the reaction mixture, carbon dots were allowed to extract to the organic layer. The carbon dots were obtained by dialysis and the excess reagents were removed. Thus, obtained carbon dots were vacuum dried at 50 °C before use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of HNT with multifunctional carbon dots (HNT-CD)
ce:section.ce:para: 0.05 g of prepared carbon dots were dissolved in a minimum quantity of ethanol, the solution was made to 100 mL with double distilled water. Thus, a prepared solution was mixed with 0.95 g of HNT and subjected to ultrasonication for a period of 20 min to get homogeneous dispersion. Further to functionalize the HNT, the dispersion was refluxed for a period of 2 h using a regular Liebig water condenser at a constant temperature of 100 ± 2 °C. Thus, obtained solid mass was separated by centrifugation, washed several times with water and ethanol, further vacuum dried at 50 °C and stored under desiccation till further use.

To the 10 mL of aqueous HNT solution (10 mg of HNT was dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water),5 mL of cisplatin drug solution is added dropwise in continuous stirring condition placed magnetic stirrer at room temperature. The stirring is continued for 24 h and cisplatin-loaded HNT was obtained by centrifuging at 4000 rpm (Remi, C-24BL, India) for 10 min, the pellet obtained was lyophilized (Alpha 1–4S, Martin Christ GmbH, Germany) and used for further experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of cisplatin loaded functionalized HNT-CD (cis@HNT-CD)
ce:section.ce:para: HNT-CD of 10 mg was dissolved in 10 mL of 0.1% of DMSO, 5 mL of cisplatin drug solution and followed the same procedure as given under synthesis of cis@HNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: of encapsulation efficiency (EE) and loading capacity (LC)
ce:section.ce:para:
Freshly prepared cisplatin-loaded HNT and HNT-CD were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min and washed twice by ultrapure water. The number of free cisplatin present in HNT and HNT-CD were recorded by performing OPDA method using UV-visible spectroscopy (Shimadzu 1800) at wavelength 730 nm respectively. EE (%) gives information about the percentage of cisplatin successfully encapsulated in HNT and HCD; it was calculated using formula (1). LC (%) deals with the nanoparticles after their separation from the medium and to know their drug content. It is calculated using formula (2) [19]. Triplicate test (n = 3) analysis of drug-loaded in HNT and HNT-CD are reported in the results and discussion section.
(1)
EE
( % )
= A − B / A * 100
(2)
LC
( % )
= A − B / C * 100
Where A is the total amount of drug, B is the free drug present in supernatant and C is the weight of cisplatin present in HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite.

Release of drugs from HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite was performed in three different pH buffers (pH 3 acetate buffers, pH 5.5 acetate buffer, pH 7.0-phosphate buffer). The freeze-dried HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite were suspended in 0.5 mL of buffer solution and transferred to a dialysis membrane, incubated in 10 mL different pH- buffer reservoirs at 37 °C with the mild shaking experimental set up was covered with foil to avoid evaporation. At determined time intervals, 1 mL of the sample was withdrawn and replaced with an equal amount of buffer. The amount of drug(s) released from HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite was found using a standard plot constructed by performing OPDA method. The experiments were carried out in triplicates to obtain the statistical analysis. Based on the drug release data obtained various Mathematical modeling was carried out to determine the order of drug release.

Cytotoxicity was determined by conducting MTT [3 (4,5dimethyl thiazol-2-yl-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide)] assay which is based on viable cells reducing soluble yellow into insoluble blue formazan product. The monolayer cell culture was trypsinized and the cell count was adjusted to 1.0 × 105 cells/mL were plated in 200 µL of DMEM medium containing 10% of Fetes Bovine Serum (FBS). After 24 h the partial monolayer is formed the supernatant is flicked off and different concentrations of synthesized formulations were added and incubated for 24 h. After the incubation time, the supernatant is taken 50 µL of MTT (5 mg/10 mL of MTT in PBS) was added to each well. The plates were gently shaken and incubated for 4 h at 37 °C in 5% of CO2 atmosphere. The supernatant was removed and 100 µL of 0.1% DMSO was added and the plates were gently shaken to solubilize the formed formazon. The absorbance was measured using a microplate reader at a wavelength of 590 nm. The percentage of growth inhibition was calculated using the following formula and (IC50) values were generated from the dose-response curves for each cell line [20].
%
of
Inhibition =
OD
of
sample
OD
of
control
× 10

To understand the interaction pattern of the HNT-CDs hybrid with Ct-DNA the spectroscopic titration (using UV-Visible and Fluorescence spectrophotometry) method was used. Stock solutions of Ct-DNA were prepared according to the reported method [18] and incubated at 4 °C for 12 h. The absorption spectrum was recorded in the range of 280–600 nm by maintaining a fixed concentration of HNT-CD, while varying the concentrations of Ct-DNA at pH 7.4 using Tris–HCl buffer. Fluorescence quenching spectra were acquired by incremental addition of HNT-CD to ethidium bromide (EB) pre-treated Ct-DNA in Tris–HCl buffer (pH=7.4) at 25 °C.

C-D spectral measurements of Ct-DNA (50 mM) were carried out on Jasco-715 spectropolarimeter in the absence and presence of HNT-CD hybrid by varying concentrations of 50 and 100 mM at room temperature. Spectra were recorded in the range of 200–600 nm having a bandwidth of 1 nm and 0.5 s time-per-point. After an average of four scans, the final C-D spectrum was generated.

An X-ray diffraction was deployed to study the crystal structure and lattice parameter of the CD, HNT and HNT-CD and is given in Fig. 1 . The XRD pattern of CDs shows a low-intensity reflection around 20° corresponds to the amorphous carbon phase in a considerably random fashion. As shown in Fig. 1 Halloysite-10 Å, which included the (001) peak at 2θ of 11.91°, (020) peak at 2θ of 20.17° indicative of the tubular halloysite structure, and the (002) peak at 2θ of 24.8° (JCPDS 29- 1487). A tiny peak appears at 2θ of 12.36° which can be ascribed to halloysite- (7A˚). The characteristic peaks of quartz (SiO2) and alumite (KAl3(SO4)2(OH)6) could also be found in the XRD patterns, showing d values of 3.34 A˚ and 2.98 A˚, respectively.

To establish the identity of surface functional groups of the CD and HNT-CD, FTIR spectroscopy has been examined (Fig. 2 ). The broad absorption in the 3160− 3360 cm−1 region can also be assigned to the N − H stretching vibration, while the sharp bands centered at 1657 and 1561 cm−1 are due to C = O stretching and N − H bending vibration of amide group. In the FTIR spectrum of HNTs, absorption bands at 3701 and 3628 cm−1 are ascribed to −OH groups. The band at 910 cm−1 is assigned to the bending vibration of Al−OH. Others bands at 1000−1100 and 450−550 cm−1 are due to the Si−O stretching and Si−O bending vibrations, respectively. Further, the spectrum of cis@HNT-CD exhibit band at 1351 cm−1 is attributed to the bending frequency of Pt-N-H and the additional band at 600 cm−1 due to the association of Pt-O confirms the proper loading of cisplatin to HNT-CD nanocomposite [21]. This confirms the covalent binding of cisplatin to HNT-CD nanocomposite.

The morphology of the pure HNT and HNT-CD has been explored by using the electron microscopic technique. The SEM of HNT in Fig. 3 a clearly shows the tubular structure as expected and the length of the tubular structure may vary from 0.5 μm to 1 μm with the diameter ranges up to ∼50 nm. Scanning transmission microscopy (STM) was used to understand the size and morphology of carbon dots (Fig. 3b), from the image it is evident that the particle size was 6–7 nm with uniform spherical morphology. The presence of crowded multifunctional dodecyl groups centered with carbon dots is visualized. The absence of lattice fringes indicates carbon dots are amorphous in nature which is the characteristic feature of CDs prepared via chemical route. After the functionalization of CD, HNTs are entangled with each other and bent with the CD. Strongback reflection of electrons as observed in the FESEM image (Fig. 3c) indicate the decoration of multifunctional carbon dots over the surface of HNT. Also, HNT-CD probably consists of short and stubby tubes with lengths ranging from 100 nm to 2 μm, while some long and thin tubes with lengths up to 3 μm were also observed.

The Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (EDX) of the HNT-CD given in Fig. S1 (supplementary) confirms the presence of C, O and N in multifunctional CD. Functionalization of multifunctional carbon dots is confirmed by the presence of C, O and N along with Al and Si of HN (Fig. 4 a). Fig. 4b exhibits additional platinum peaks that approves the cisplatin drug has been successfully loaded to HNT-CD nanocomposite.
The absorption and emission spectra of CD is given in the supplementary information (Fig. S2), indicating absorption at ∼ 350 nm and 450 nm and emission at 510 nm.

The EE and LC of cisplatin with HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite have been evaluated and are given in Fig. 5 . From the figure, the EE and LC of cisplatin loaded with HNT were found to be 81% and 41%, whereas it is found to be 96% and 62% with HNT-CD nanocomposite. When compared to HNT, the EE and LC of HNT-CD nanocomposite exhibit enhanced capacity due to functionalization of HNT-CD that could firmly bound for selective encapsulation and loading of cisplatin.

The release of cisplatin from HNT has been evaluated at three different pH and is given in Fig. 6 a. At all pH the initial burst release was observed followed by sustained release because the drug molecules are bound on the external surface of halloysites. At pH 3.0 ∼60% drug was released for 30 min, 72% at 6 h, reached to ∼78.8% at 24 h. At pH 5.5 ∼40% of the drug was released at 5 h, 66% at 24 h. At pH 7.4 ∼20% was released for 30 min and later reached 60% at 24 h. Sustained release of drug from halloysite was due to the presence of the clay, which was hypothesized to form a consistent layer around the drug-loaded halloysite during the formation of nanoformulations. The release of drug from HNT is faster at acidic medium due to swelling characteristic of the matrix of HNT. This release profile of drug-loaded halloysite was moderately linear indicating the constant release of drug over time which is a characteristic behavior for a sustained drug. The pH-responsive release raised the possibility that the cationic nature of the drug caused it to interact electrostatically with the negatively charged external surfaces of HNT and thus are held relatively tightly to the HNTs. The principle of electrostatic binding to a negatively charged particle surface has been well discussed in silica-based nanocarriers. [22,23]. The release of cisplatin from HNT-CD nanocomposite was also evaluated by three different pH as shown in Fig. 6b. At all pH the initial burst release was observed as discussed above. At pH 3.0 ∼40% drug was released for 30 min and reached ∼88% at 24 h. At pH 5.5 ∼40% of the drug was released at 5 h, 75% at 24 h. At pH 7.4 ∼30% was released for 30 min and later reached 60% at 24 h. At all pH, the release of drug was more when compared to drug-loaded HNT, because of surface modification. Surface modification of HNTs by CD plays an important role in the formation of agglomerates. Table 1 shows the regression coefficients derived for various mathematical models used to correlate the release kinetics of drugs from HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite in different release media. From the regression values, it was observed that there was good agreement with Korsmeyer-Peppas, zero-order kinetics in all release media indicating a quantity of drug released is proportional to the amount of drug present in the carrier and the release is controlled by a combination of diffusion and surface erosion.

To determine the cell viability of synthesized formulations the in-vitro cytotoxicity test was carried out against MCF-7 cell lines. Cell viability was carried out in a dose-dependent manner for 24 has shown in the Fig. 7 . The results showed that HNT was nontoxic even at the highest concentrations 100 µg mL−1 proving the safety performance of HNT acts a potent delivery of drugs (the IC50 value was 100%). These results were in good agreement with HNT based drug delivery system, non-toxicity, good cell membrane interaction, biocompatible, biodegradable properties [24,25].

HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite as such and in presence of cisplatin has been evaluated for its cytotoxic studies. The surface-modified HNT with CD did not show any cytotoxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines proving that surface modification of HNT by CD by this method acts as the carrier of drugs, does not alter the cytotoxicity of HNT (the IC50 value was 100%). Cisplatin-loaded HNT showed the cytotoxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines in a dose-dependent manner (The toxicity was initially 85.12% and later reached 40% as the concentration increased-the IC50 value was 84.85%). HNT can encapsulate drugs and release the drugs to the target site [26]. Cisplatin loaded surface HNT-CD showed greater toxicity against MCF-7 breast cancer cell lines as the concentration increased the cell viability has decreased. The surface modification increases the size decreases the surface area of the HNT, which provides better encapsulation efficiency of drugs, improving the stability, cellular uptake, prolonged circulating time distribution, and metabolism of drugs (the IC50 value was 41%). CD-based surface modification of HNT improves the bioavailability of drugs and releases the drugs to a specific site [27,28].

UV-Visible spectroscopy is the most commonly used method for detecting and distinguishing associations between materials with biomolecules and complex structures. The intensity of association was correlated with the degree of absorbance or transition in the peak position. In the absence and presence of an increasing concentration of Ct-DNA, the absorption spectra of HNT-CD nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 8 . The intercalation mode between molecules and DNA is typically associated with hypochromism while a hyperchromic mode is connected with groove binding between host and guest [29]. A decrease in the absorption of the hybrid HNT- CD (hypochromism) was observed by the addition of Ct-DNA that suggests the intercalation of the HNT-CD nanocomposite with Ct-DNA. Depending upon the binding constant using the classical Vanthoff Eq. (4), the Gibbs free energy (∆G) of test compounds and DNA was determined.
(4)
Δ
G = − RTlnKb
Here R=Universal gas constant (8.314 kJ K − 1mol−1) and T=Temperature (298 K). The ∆G value for the HNT-CD nanocomposite was found to be −27.91 KJ mol−1 indicates the spontaneous binding process between the test compound and Ct-DNA. Further experiments have been performed for the study of HNT-CD nanocomposite binding mode with Ct-DNA.

Fluorescence spectroscopy is an essential tool and is the most commonly used to analyze the binding kinetics of compounds with Ct-DNA. The fluorescence emission spectra of HNT-CD nanocomposite with Ct-DNA bound EB are seen in Fig. 9 a. Upon subsequent addition of HNT-CD nanocomposite, the emission intensity decreased dramatically without any significant changes in emission maxima Data from the emission studies reveals that HNT-CD nanocomposite binds to Ct-DNA via intercalative mode.

To determine the quenching constant in the context of binding interactions, the classical Stern-Volmer equation [30] was used. The Stern-Volmer plots of F0/F versus [Q] are seen in Fig. 9b. The quenching constant (Ksv) was 1.355×104 L mol−1, suggesting that the HNT-CD nanocomposite interacts with Ct-DNA strongly and probably via the intercalative binding mode. Furthermore, the values of biomolecular quenching rate constant (Kq, 1.352×1012 L mol−1 s − 1) were found to be greater than (in the range of 10 12 M − 1s−1) the fluorescence lifetime of the biomolecule (10−8 s), which suggests that the quenching process could be static [31]. The magnitude of the binding constant (Kb) and the number of binding sites (n) was determined from Eq. (5).
(5)
log
( F O − F )
/ F = l o g K b + nlog
[ Q ]
where, F0 and F denote the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of the quencher, respectively, and [Q] is the concentration of quencher. The plot of log[(F0−F)/F]versus log [Q] was shown in Fig. 9c. Typically, the Kb value ranging from 104 to108 L M − 1 is a strong binding affinity [32]. The linear plot in Fig. 9c provides a binding constant of 7.809×104 L mol−1 for HNT-CD nanocomposite in present studies. The sequence of the binding constant analyzed indicates that the probe is intercalated with the DNA environment. The n value was very close to 1, suggesting that a single binding site exists between the HNT-CD hybrid and Ct- DNA.

C-D is a technique that is employed to explore conformational modifications in DNA with small molecules. Free Ct-DNA C-D spectra show a positive band at 275 nm, which is typical for base stacking and a negative band at 245 nm due to right-handed helicity [33], which are a characteristic of the B-form of DNA (Fig. 10 ). The change in the intensity of these spectral bands is attributed to the resulting conformational changes in the duplex DNA correlated with the drug interaction. The HNT-CD nanocomposite has decreased the intensity of both positive as well as negative bands relative to Ct-DNA owing to strong attraction leading to change of the helical form of DNA and base stacking. This finding agrees with the results of the absorption and emission studies which indicate association with intercalative binding mode of HNT-CD hybrid with Ct-DNA.

Multifunctional carbon dots have been synthesized by the chemical route and functionalized successfully with HNT clay material to generate multifunctional HNT-CD nanocomposite. Cisplatin drug has also been loaded efficiently to HNT and HNT-CD nanocomposite and exhibited sustained release in both cases. HNT-CD nanocomposite loaded with cisplatin exhibited enhanced cytotoxicity against MCF-7 cells compared to HNT loaded cisplatin. This indicates, CD play a vital role in its cytotoxicity studies. HNT-CD nanocomposite was also evaluated for its DNA binding studies using fluorescence spectrophotometer and circular dichroism techniques. From the results obtained, one can conclude HNT functionalized CD could serve as an alternate potential material in the field of biomedicine and it will give a path for future researchers to explore a new way of biomedical research and development.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors are immensely elated and wish to express their indebted gratitude to the Management of their respective Institutes for providing lab facilities to carry out this work. Authors extend their thanks and appreciation to Researchers Supporting Project (Ref: RSP-2021/160) King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia. Also, this research was supported by Basic Science Research Programthrough the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by Ministry of Education (2021R1A6A1A03043682).
